1_tldr.title: TL;DR / Executive Summary
1_tldr.coverage: Federal HIPAA Privacy/Security/Breach + BAA control-map seed; state medical-privacy add-ons for CA, TX, NY.
1_tldr.honesty_rule: Every claim STD-CITE-001 cited; gaps render NOT FOUND — [source checked: …]; eCFR primary when HHS 403.

2_scope_methodology_as_of.title: Scope, Methodology & As-Of Date
2_scope_methodology_as_of.methodology: Public-source harvest 2026-06-19. Primary federal authority = eCFR 45 CFR Parts 160 & 164. HHS.gov HIPAA guidance pages PARKED (HTTP 403). State statutes via official legislature portals; SPA/Cloudflare gaps flagged explicitly.

federal_hipaa_summary.text: HIPAA covered entities (health plans, clearinghouses, providers transmitting standard electronic transactions) and their business associates must comply with Privacy, Security, and Breach Notification rules codified at 45 CFR Part 164.
PHI may not be used or disclosed except as permitted or required; minimum necessary applies to most uses and requests.
BAAs must document permitted uses, require safeguards for ePHI, mandate breach reporting, flow down subcontractor obligations, and authorize termination for material breach.
Security Rule administrative safeguards require risk analysis, designated security official, incident response, and contingency planning (backup, disaster recovery, emergency mode).
Technical safeguards require access control, audit controls, integrity, authentication, and transmission security for ePHI.
Breach of unsecured PHI: notify affected individuals within 60 days of discovery; business associates must notify covered entities within 60 days.

key_requirements: Authorization required before disclosure except listed compelled/permitted cases (§56.10).
No sale/marketing/non-treatment use of medical information (§56.10(d)).
Authorization form: 14-point type, specific uses, 1-year max expiration (§56.11).
HIPAA Part 164 cross-reference for ERISA plan disclosures (§56.10(c)(21)).
Chapter anchor confirmed; section-level disclosure/authorization rules REQUIRES_CONFIRMATION (SPA portal).
NY DOH: state laws more stringent than HIPAA are not superseded; preemption charts map PHL vs. HIPAA.
PHL §17 patient-directed record transfer prevails as required-by-law disclosure.
PHL §18 practitioner patient-information rules differ in scope from HIPAA record sets.
SHIELD Act statute text and AG breach portal NOT FOUND at harvest — REQUIRES_CONFIRMATION.

4_source_register_graded.title: Source Register (Graded)
4_source_register_graded.summary: eCFR = primary federal; CA leginfo = primary state; NY DOH = secondary preemption analysis; HHS/TX SPA/nysenate = parked or restricted.

items.assessment: eCFR used as operative primary; HHS guidance PARKED until portal accessible. No substantive conflict detected in harvested CFR text.
CMIA explicitly references HIPAA Part 164 for certain ERISA disclosures; CMIA authorization and anti-sale rules may be more stringent — apply stricter standard.
NY DOH documents instances where PHL §17/§18 prevail as more stringent or required-by-law; dual-track compliance required for NY practitioners.

7_confidence.title: Confidence Assessment
7_confidence.basis: Federal HIPAA controls well-sourced via eCFR (8 domains, 31 FOUND controls). California CMIA fully harvested. Texas section text and NY SHIELD primary statute gaps reduce state-addon confidence.
